source.title: Fatal Combustible Dust Explosions at Didion Milling Inc.

event.top_event: Combustible dust explosions and building collapse
actions_taken: Employees investigated source of smoke in mill process areas
Employees began evacuation upon hearing explosion
Employees attempted to notify other employees using radios
event.summary: On May 31, 2017, at approximately 11:00 p.m., multiple combustible dust explosions occurred at the Didion Milling dry corn milling facility in Cambria, Wisconsin. The incident fatally injured five employees and injured 14 others. During the day of the incident, Didion connected the discharge of two rotary gap mills to increase production. On the night of the incident, employees smelled smoke and investigated the source. A fire spread through process equipment via interconnected dust collection systems, resulting in explosions in dust collection equipment. Secondary explosions occurred throughout the facility, causing the collapse of multiple buildings including the Multipurpose Building, F Mill, and the Boiler House. The estimated property damage was $15,375,959 with no off-site consequences.
recommendations: Develop comprehensive combustible dust process safety management system including management of change, process safety information, audits and inspections, fugitive dust management, incident investigation, dust hazard analyses, engineering controls, PPE, and emergency preparedness
Modify pneumatic conveying and dust collector ductwork systems to ensure minimum transport velocity and implement periodic inspection and testing programs
Perform dust hazard analyses on all buildings and units processing combustible dust with revalidation every five years
Assess and implement engineering controls for structural design and venting of reconstructed facility
Incorporate paper-based process safety information into electronic records management system
Perform personal protective equipment hazard analyses and require flame-resistant garments for combustible dust operations
Update facility emergency response plan with signal/alarm system, emergency shutdown procedures, evacuation instructions, and response to fire scenarios
Assess and update pre-deflagration detection and suppression engineering controls
Develop and implement process safety leadership and culture program
Issue comprehensive OSHA combustible dust standard based on NFPA standards

threats.name: Smoldering fire in gap mill equipment
Ignition of dust cloud in process equipment

consequences.name: Building collapse (Multipurpose Building, F Mill, Boiler House)
Structural damage to D Mill and B Mill

controls.name: Process Hazard Recognition and Assessment
controls.barrier_role: Identify combustible dust hazards in process streams

supporting_text: Didion did not accurately assess a number of process streams in the mill buildings that contained combustible dust hazards
The failure to recognize that interconnecting equipment through dust collectors could present a deflagration propagation hazard
The incorrect conclusion, using incorrect calculations, that the dust collectors in the mill facility did not contain explosive dust concentrations

controls.name: Dust Hazard Analysis (DHA)
controls.barrier_role: Systematically assess combustible dust hazards and identify required controls

supporting_text: While the NFPA guidance was published in 2015, Didion had not performed any analyses of the grain handling processes for potential fire and explosion hazards
To date, the Grain Handling Facilities Standard, 29 CFR § 1910.272, does not require a DHA for compliance

controls.name: Deflagration Venting
controls.barrier_role: Relieve pressure from deflagration to prevent building collapse
controls.lod_basis: NFPA 68 guidance on explosion protection by deflagration venting

supporting_text: The mill buildings were not designed to relieve the pressure generated from a combustible dust explosion

The mill buildings lacked deflagration vents that could relieve pressure and mitigate the damage that occurred due to the propagation of the pressure wave through the mill from secondary dust explosions

controls.name: Deflagration Suppression
controls.barrier_role: Detect and suppress deflagration before pressure buildup
controls.lod_basis: NFPA 69 guidance on explosion prevention systems

supporting_text: The lack of engineering controls for the control and mitigation of combustible dust fires and explosions throughout the Didion milling facility allowed the propagation of the fire, resulting in deaths, injuries, and damage to the facility

controls.name: Deflagration Isolation
controls.barrier_role: Prevent deflagration propagation between interconnected equipment

supporting_text: A fire, a type of deflagration, spread through the process equipment via interconnected dust collection systems resulting in explosions in some of the dust collection equipment

controls.name: Ductwork Design and Maintenance
controls.barrier_role: Maintain proper transport velocity and prevent material accumulation in ductwork
controls.lod_basis: NFPA 654 and NFPA 91 guidance on pneumatic conveying systems

supporting_text: The failure to recognize that proper design and verification of ductwork system sizing and performance are crucial for safe dust collector operation and pneumatic conveying
The failure to correctly design and maintain these systems allowed combustible material to accumulate inside ductwork systems, contributing to the incident's severity
Dry Grit Filter supply ducting post-incident with corn product accumulation

controls.name: Fugitive Dust Management
controls.barrier_role: Control and remove accumulated dust to prevent secondary explosions
controls.lod_basis: NFPA 654 and NFPA 61 guidance on housekeeping

supporting_text: Didion's use of food safety standards to address combustible dust hazards created gaps in process safety at the mill facility, particularly for combustible dust propagation hazards and around dust collectors
Examples of fugitive dust accumulations

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC)
controls.barrier_role: Assess process changes for combustible dust hazards before implementation

supporting_text: During the day of the incident, Didion connected the discharge of two rotary gap mills in an attempt to increase production
While Didion had a Management of Change program as part of the food safety program, the program did not assess changes for combustible dust hazards
Didion's weak management of change program allowed changes to occur in the processes without the evaluation of potential hazards

controls.name: Incident Investigation Program
controls.barrier_role: Investigate prior incidents to identify and correct hazards

supporting_text: Prior to the incident on May 31, 2017, Didion had experienced several fires and smoldering material incidents that could have resulted in more significant fires and explosions

Didion's failure to thoroughly investigate prior incidents involving fires and smoldering events prevented Didion from implementing corrective actions that could prevent or mitigate fires and explosions within the mill facility
The failure to address the causes of the prior incidents indicated a weak incident investigation program

controls.name: Process Safety Information Management
controls.barrier_role: Maintain and retain critical safety documentation for decision-making

supporting_text: Prior to the incident, Didion did not maintain key process safety information, such as deflagration engineering control design documentation, dust collector design information, or dust explosibility data
The lack of this critical information allowed Didion to make changes to the process without consideration of the potential hazards

controls.name: External Audits and Inspections Management
controls.barrier_role: Address hazards identified by external parties to prevent incidents
controls.lod_basis: OSHA PSM and industry best practices

supporting_text: In the years leading up to the incident, Didion underwent several audits and inspections by external parties, including the Occupational Safety Health Administration (OSHA) and Didion's insurance carrier. These inspections identified the potential for combustible dust hazards, including propagation of fire resulting in multiple explosions and collapse of the buildings
The failure to address the hazards identified by external parties as well as the turnover of responsible management staff left the facility at risk of fires and explosions

controls.name: Emergency Preparedness and Response
controls.barrier_role: Notify employees of emergency and enable safe evacuation
controls.lod_basis: NFPA 61 and NFPA 652 guidance on emergency preparedness

supporting_text: Employees began to evacuate and attempted to notify other employees of the emergency using their radios; however, conflicting radio traffic caused confusion with the intended message
The employees who were not within the immediate vicinity of the observed fire were unaware of the emergency and the need to evacuate prior to the explosions
Didion's emergency response plan failed to adequately prescribe actions to be taken by employees and management during fire incidents
Didion lacked a facility-wide notification system that could immediately notify all employees of the need to evacuate or of an ongoing emergency

controls.name: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
controls.barrier_role: Protect employees from thermal exposure during flash fires and explosions

supporting_text: Didion did not require the use of fire-resistant clothing within the milling facility, although Didion previously experienced a combustible dust flash fire that resulted in an employee suffering burn injuries

The personal protective equipment prescribed by Didion did not protect the employees from the flames they were exposed to during the explosions and propagation that occurred on the night of the incident

controls.name: Process Safety Leadership and Culture
controls.barrier_role: Establish organizational commitment to identify and mitigate hazards
controls.lod_basis: CCPS Guidelines for Process Safety Management

supporting_text: Didion's poor safety culture and inadequate leadership on safety issues allowed the normalization of deviance regarding smoldering fires, a lack of deference to expertise, a lack of a sense of vulnerability, and inadequate understanding of the combustible dust and deflagration hazards present at Didion

The combination of these factors and decisions made by Didion leadership contributed to the inadequate engineering controls, over-reliance on administrative controls, and inadequate emergency response planning at the time of the incident
Due to the number of weaknesses in the implementation and management of safety programs, Didion exhibited a lack of safety leadership and a poor safety culture

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
